title: rtk err loses argument quoting (breaks node -e / python3 -c) and exits 0 on failure
author: cboydstun
body:
## Description

`rtk err <cmd>` appears to re-join its argv into a single string and hand it to `sh -c`, which loses the original argument quoting. Any argument containing shell metacharacters — most commonly `node -e '...'` or `python3 -c '...'` — is re-split by the shell and fails with a syntax error.

Worse, the failure is **reported as exit 0**, so a caller (or an agent) sees a "successful" run that never actually executed the command.

## Repro steps

```bash
rtk err node -e 'console.log("a")'
```

Actual:

```
sh: -c: line 0: syntax error near unexpected token `('
sh: -c: line 0: `node -e console.log("a")'
```

```bash
echo $?   # -> 0
```

Note the echoed command in the error: the single quotes around `console.log("a")` are gone, so `sh` sees the parentheses as unquoted metacharacters.

Running the same command directly works fine:

```bash
node -e 'console.log("a")'   # -> a
```

## Expected

Two things:

1. `rtk err` should preserve argument boundaries — exec the argv directly rather than reconstructing a shell string (or shell-quote each argument before passing to `sh -c`).
2. It should propagate the child's exit code. A command that failed to run must not exit 0.

## Actual

Quoting is lost, the command never runs, and the exit status is 0.

## Impact

`rtk err` is the recommended wrapper for commands RTK has no dedicated filter for — which is exactly the population of commands most likely to carry `-e` / `-c` / quoted arguments. The silent exit-0 makes this especially bad for agents, which will treat the failure as a success.

## Version / environment

- rtk 0.43.0, macOS arm64 (Darwin 24.6.0)

## Note

The exit-code half may share a root cause with #2974 (`pnpm`/`eslint` exit code swallowed); the quoting loss looks independent.

title: hook: --ultra-compact and --skip-env are accepted but never propagate into the rewritten command
author: cboydstun
body:
## Description

`rtk hook claude` accepts `--ultra-compact` and `--skip-env` (they're documented in `rtk hook --help`), but neither flag has any effect: the rewritten command emitted to Claude Code is byte-identical with and without them. The flags are silently dropped.

This matters because the hook is the *only* path most Claude Code users ever exercise — commands are rewritten transparently, so a user never types `rtk` by hand. Configuring `"command": "rtk hook claude --ultra-compact"` in `~/.claude/settings.json` looks like it enables Level 2 savings, but it is a no-op. There is no error and no warning, so the setting appears to work.

## Repro steps

```bash
for f in "" "--ultra-compact" "--skip-env"; do
  echo '{"tool_name":"Bash","tool_input":{"command":"next build"}}' \
    | rtk hook claude $f \
    | python3 -c 'import sys,json;print(json.load(sys.stdin)["hookSpecificOutput"]["updatedInput"]["command"])'
done
```

Output:

```
rtk next     # no flag
rtk next     # --ultra-compact
rtk next     # --skip-env
```

Same result for every command type I tried (`git status`, `jest`, `tsc --noEmit`, `eslint src`, `grep -rn foo src`, `npm run build`).

## Expected

The hook should propagate its flags into the rewritten command, e.g.

```
rtk hook claude --ultra-compact   ->  rtk next --ultra-compact
rtk hook claude --skip-env        ->  rtk next --skip-env
```

`rtk next` (and the other subcommands) already accept both flags — `rtk next --help` lists them — so the target side is in place; only the hook's rewrite is missing them.

If the flags are intentionally unsupported on the hook path, they should be rejected at parse time or removed from `rtk hook --help` rather than accepted and ignored.

## Actual

Flags are accepted, produce no change, and emit no warning.

## Version / environment

- rtk 0.43.0, macOS arm64 (Darwin 24.6.0)
- Hook configured as a `PreToolUse` / `Bash` matcher in `~/.claude/settings.json`

## Note

Distinct from #2369, which is about the top-level `-u` shorthand not being implemented on the main CLI. This report is about the long flags being accepted by `rtk hook claude` and then not reaching the rewritten command.

title: [Wrong repo] Close
author: wibu009
body: Hey team, Found a breaking telemetry bug in agent-host-copilotcli that only happens when using the kimi-k2.7-code model. Other models seem to track just fine. Basically, the token tracking is completely dead, which then cascades and breaks the Cache Explorer dashboard. 1. Zero Token Telemetry Bug As shown in the first screenshot, the agent successfully runs through 17 turns and 24 tool calls with 0 errors. However, all token metrics (Total Input, Total Output, Total Cached, Total Tokens) are completely stuck at 0. The execution works fine, but the telemetry data is missing. 2. Broken Cache Explorer (Cascading Error) Because the token counts are missing, the Cache Explorer is completely blind. It shows 0.00% cache hit and throws a fake "Likely cache expiration (~5 min)" warning. But if you look closely at the timestamps, the gap between Turn 15 and Turn 16 is only 13 seconds (Turn 15 ended at 07:09:51 and Turn 16 started at 07:10:04). There is no way the prompt cache expired in 13 seconds. Both input tok and cached tok fields are completely blank. It looks like the CLI is either failing to parse the token usage block from Kimi's API response, or it's completely forgetting to pass the caching headers for this specific model.

title: pnpm/eslint exit code swallowed — and 'rtk proxy' swallows it too
author: verveguy
body:
## Summary

`rtk pnpm lint` (eslint under pnpm) prints the failure output — including pnpm's own `ELIFECYCLE Command failed with exit code 1` — but **exits 0**. More surprisingly, **`rtk proxy pnpm lint` also exits 0**, even though `proxy` is documented as "execute raw command without filtering". Meanwhile trivial commands propagate correctly, so this is specific to the recognized-tool path, not general exit handling.

Related: #2735 (same class, golangci-lint), #2658 (pnpm outdated). This report adds the `rtk proxy` data point — the escape hatch users would reach for to avoid exactly this bug doesn't escape it.

## Environment

- rtk 0.43.0 (Homebrew)
- pnpm 10.x, eslint 10.1.0 (project-local)
- macOS (darwin, arm64)

## Reproduce

In any pnpm project whose `lint` script is eslint, with at least one lint error present:

```sh
$ pnpm lint; echo "raw: $?"
# ✖ 1 problem (1 error, 0 warnings)
#  ELIFECYCLE  Command failed with exit code 1.
# raw: 1

$ rtk pnpm lint; echo "rtk: $?"
# ✖ 1 problem (1 error, 0 warnings)
#  ELIFECYCLE  Command failed with exit code 1.
# rtk: 0        ← swallowed

$ rtk proxy pnpm lint; echo "proxy: $?"
# (same output)
# proxy: 0      ← swallowed even by proxy
```

Control — generic commands propagate fine:

```sh
$ rtk proxy sh -c 'exit 3'; echo $?   # → 3 ✓
$ rtk proxy false; echo $?            # → 1 ✓
```

## Impact

The Claude Code hook auto-rewrites common dev commands through rtk, so **every exit-code-based gate lies**: `&&` chains, pre-commit hooks, CI-parity checks, and AI agents verifying "did lint pass?" by exit status. Concretely: an agent supervising this repo shipped a commit that broke CI because its local `pnpm lint` verification printed the errors but reported exit 0 — the failure was only caught when GitHub Actions (running the same command un-wrapped) went red.

## Expected

rtk should always exit with the child's exit code (or 128+signal), in both filtered and `proxy` modes. Token-optimizing the *output* should never alter the *status*.

title: Support commands wrapped in 'uv run' (e.g. 'uv run pytest', 'uv run ruff check')
author: Andi777x
body:
## Problem

`rtk discover` flags `uv run` as the single largest unhandled command pattern in my project (a `uv`-managed Python 3.12 codebase): **540+ occurrences over 30 days, ~significant estimated tokens saveable** — larger than any other gap in the report.

The underlying subcommands are frequently ones rtk *already* has native support for, e.g.:

```
uv run pytest ...
uv run ruff check .
uv run mypy src/
uv run alembic heads
```

But because the invocation is prefixed with `uv run`, rtk doesn't recognize/rewrite it and falls back to a raw passthrough (confirmed via `rtk gain --history`, which shows entries like `rtk proxy uv run pytest ... -0% (0)` — zero savings, since it's just passed through instead of routed to the existing `rtk pytest` filter).

## Suggested fix

Treat `uv run <cmd> ...` (and ideally `uv run --frozen <cmd>`, `uv run --no-sync <cmd>`, etc.) as a transparent prefix: strip it, then match `<cmd> ...` against the existing rewrite registry the same way a bare invocation would be matched. This would immediately unlock existing filters (`rtk pytest`, `rtk ruff check`, `rtk mypy` if/when supported, etc.) for the entire `uv`-based Python ecosystem without needing new per-tool logic — `uv run` is the standard way `uv`-managed projects (increasingly common per PEP 621 / pyproject.toml-based tooling) invoke their dev commands.

Similar prefixes that might benefit from the same "unwrap and re-match" treatment: `poetry run <cmd>`, `pipenv run <cmd>` — happy to open separate issues if useful, but wanted to start with `uv run` since it's by far the highest-volume case I'm seeing.

## Environment

- rtk 0.43.0 (Homebrew)
- Detected via `rtk discover --all --since 30`

title: Unwrap `poetry run <tool>` before registry lookup (408 unhandled calls/30d — top unhandled command)
author: voliveira-mediaocean
body:
### Summary

`rtk rewrite` does not recognize commands wrapped in `poetry run <tool>`, so they never reach the registry and get no filtering. This is the sibling of #1205 (`uv run` / `uvx`) for the other major Python dependency manager, and of #2921 (npm/pnpm/yarn) on the Node side.

### Reproduction (rtk 0.43.0, macOS)

```
$ rtk --version
rtk 0.43.0

$ rtk rewrite "poetry run pytest tests/"        ; echo "exit=$?"
exit=1                                          # no RTK equivalent

$ rtk rewrite "poetry run ruff check ."         ; echo "exit=$?"
exit=1

$ rtk rewrite "poetry run black ."              ; echo "exit=$?"
exit=1
```

rtk already has the handlers (`pytest` via the test path, `format` for black/ruff). Only the `poetry run` prefix stops the lookup.

### Why it's worth handling

`poetry run` is the **most frequent unhandled command** in my `rtk discover` output — 408 calls in the last 30 days, ahead of every other unhandled entry:

```
TOP UNHANDLED COMMANDS
Command                  Count    Example
poetry run                 408    poetry run pytest "tests/test_incident..
python3                    156    ...
git checkout               101    ...
```

And the output it leaves unfiltered is almost pure noise. A real run in one of my repos, 98 tests, verbose mode on via `configfile`:

- **raw:** 109 lines, of which **98 are `PASSED` progress lines**
- **filtered:** 4 lines (`collected 98 items` + the summary)

That is ~96% of the output being noise, on the single most-invoked unhandled command.

### Proposed fix

Unwrap the `poetry run` prefix before the registry lookup, exactly as proposed for `uv run` in #1205, so `poetry run pytest ...` resolves to the pytest handler.

Important: the wrapper must be **preserved when executing**, not just stripped — `pytest` outside `poetry run` runs against the wrong interpreter (or is not on PATH at all). The command still has to execute as `poetry run pytest ...`; only the *lookup* should see through the prefix.

### Workaround

For anyone hitting this before it lands: routing the command through `rtk <cmd>` passthrough plus a global TOML filter works today and preserves the child exit code (verified: green suite → exit 0, failing suite → exit 1 with the traceback and `FAILED` lines intact).

```toml
# ~/Library/Application Support/rtk/filters.toml
[filters.pytest-wrapped]
description = "pytest via poetry/python -m: drop passing tests and session banner"
match_command = "(poetry run|python[0-9.]* -m|/bin/python[0-9.]* -m) +pytest"
strip_ansi = true
strip_lines_matching = [
  " PASSED\\s+\\[\\s*\\d+%\\]$",
  "^\\S+\\.py [.s]+\\s+\\[\\s*\\d+%\\]$",
  "^platform ",
  "^cachedir:",
  "^rootdir:",
  "^configfile:",
  "^plugins:",
  "^asyncio:",
  "^=+ test session starts =+$",
]
```

One gotcha worth documenting: `match_command` must not match a bare Rust module name. Using `match_command = "pytest"` makes rtk disable the filter with

```
[rtk] warning: filter 'pytest' match_command matches 'pytest' which is already handled by
a Rust module — this filter will never activate for that command
```

so the pattern has to be anchored on the wrapper, as above.

title: Pipe-left rewrites feed rtk's summarized output into downstream programs (grep | wc -l counts 28 for 60 matches)
author: xuing
body:
## What happens

The hook rewrites the left side of a pipe, so a *downstream program* — not the agent — ends up consuming rtk's compacted format. The compacted format has headers, caps, elision markers and decoration, and the consumer silently gets wrong data.

Reproduced on rtk 0.43.0; present in `develop` (`5d32d07`).

**`grep | wc -l` miscounts** (file has 60 matching lines):

```console
$ grep -n "match" sixty.txt | wc -l      # → rtk grep -n match sixty.txt | wc -l
28
```

28 = 1 header line (`60 matches in 1 files:`) + 1 blank + 25 shown matches + 1 elision line. The real answer is 60.

The intermediate output the pipe consumer actually sees:

```
60 matches in 1 files:

1:match line 1
…
25:match line 25
  +35 more in /.../sixty.txt [see remaining: tail -n +26 ~/.local/share/rtk/tee/…]
```

Note the path is also elided to `/.../sixty.txt`, so a consumer trying to use it as a path fails.

**Same class of problem for other shape-changing filters:**
- `ls` — `$(ls -t | head -1)` captures a size-decorated entry rather than a bare name.
- `head` → `rtk read --max-lines` — the `[N more lines]` marker reaches whatever parses the file content.

## Why this is the existing design's own rule

`find`/`fd` already have exactly this carve-out — `registry.rs` skips rewriting them when they feed a pipe, because rtk's tree output is incompatible with `xargs` (#439). The same reasoning applies to every filter whose output shape differs from the native tool's; the carve-out is just too narrow.

This also sits directly under the project's **Transparency** principle: "If an LLM parses `git diff` output, RTK's filtered version must still look like `git diff` output." When a pipe is present, the parser is a *program*, and the shape difference is fatal rather than cosmetic.

PR incoming: generalize the find/fd carve-out to a `PIPE_INCOMPATIBLE` list (grep, rg, ls, head, tail, cat) + regression tests. Non-piped uses keep their rewrites, so the token savings on the common case are untouched.

title: rtk read --max-lines N returns only N/2 lines of plain text (hook's head -N loses half the file)
author: xuing
body:
## What happens

`rtk read --max-lines N` returns at most **N/2** content lines for plain text, not N. Because the Claude hook maps `head -N` → `rtk read --max-lines N`, `head -4` shows 2 lines:

```console
$ head -4 ten.txt          # file is line1..line10 → rtk read ten.txt --max-lines 4
line1
line2
[6 more lines]
```

Reproduced on rtk 0.43.0; present in `develop` (`5d32d07`).

## Why

`core::filter::smart_truncate` only keeps a non-"important" line while `kept_lines < max_lines / 2`, and breaks the loop at `max_lines - 1`:

```rust
if is_important || kept_lines < max_lines / 2 {
    result.push((*line).to_string());
    kept_lines += 1;
}
if kept_lines >= max_lines - 1 { break; }
```

So plain text (no function signatures / imports to mark "important") caps at `max_lines / 2`.

## Why it matters

`--max-lines N` is an **explicit budget** — the caller asked for N lines. Returning half of them (per the project's "Correctness VS Token Savings" principle: explicitly-requested detail should be respected) means an agent using `head -N` to read a file gets a window inconsistent with the real file. For code files the kept lines are signature-only, so the visible "first N lines" can contain lines from much deeper in the file, breaking edit-anchor reads.

Related: #2820 (closed) covered a different head/tail rewrite defect (`tail -N` → invalid `rtk read -N`); the N/2 budget is not covered anywhere.

PR incoming: fill the budget to exactly N (important lines claim it first, earliest remaining lines fill the rest) + regression tests.

title: pytest filter reports "No tests collected" for runs that executed tests; a colored '1 failed' vanishes from the summary
author: xuing
body:
## What happens

Two distinct, unreported triggers make `rtk pytest` report **`Pytest: No tests collected` (and silently discard the collected FAILURES section) for runs that actually executed tests** — including failing ones. Both reproduced on rtk 0.43.0 and present in `develop` (`5d32d07`: the `-q` injection in `pytest_cmd.rs::run` and the zero-count early return in `build_pytest_summary`).

For an agent that reads the filtered text, this is a false green: a failing test run comes back looking like an empty one.

## Trigger 1 — the injected `-q` doubles up with the project's `addopts = -q`

`rtk pytest` unconditionally appends `-q` when the CLI args don't contain it. It cannot see the project's pytest config; with this textbook `pytest.ini`:

```ini
[pytest]
addopts = -q
```

every run is effectively `-qq` — a verbosity level at which **pytest prints no final summary line at all**. No summary line → all counts parse to zero → `"No tests collected"`, and the early return drops the FAILURES that the state machine had already collected.

```console
$ rtk pytest tests/test_contracts.py        # 20 passing tests, addopts = -q
Pytest: No tests collected
$ echo $?
0
```

Same command through `rtk proxy`: `20 passed, 1 warning in 0.04s`.

With a file containing **2 real failing tests**, the filtered run still prints `Pytest: No tests collected` and exits 0.

## Trigger 2 — ANSI-colored summaries defeat the count parser

`parse_summary_line` does `words[i-1].parse::<usize>()` on raw text. Under `FORCE_COLOR` / `PY_COLORS` / CI, pytest wraps the counts in escapes, and `\x1b[32m30` is not a number:

```console
$ printf '\x1b[32m30 passed\x1b[0m\x1b[33m, 1 warning\x1b[0m\x1b[32m in 0.11s\x1b[0m'  # fixture
→ Pytest: No tests collected
```

Worse, on a mixed line the failure count alone is dropped (its `1` carries the color prefix; `passed`'s survives after the comma split):

```console
$ printf '\x1b[31m1 failed\x1b[0m\x1b[32m, 1 passed\x1b[0m\x1b[31m in 0.05s\x1b[0m'  # fixture
→ Pytest: 1 passed          # the failure is gone from the summary
```

## Relationship to existing work

- #565 (closed) fixed the *bare quiet summary line* variant — both triggers above bypass that fix (there is no summary line at all / the line is colored).
- #2850 / #2346 (open) address the *collection error* variant of the same early return. The two triggers here are different entry points into it; fixes are orthogonal.
- #2317 (open) is the general "filters mask hard failures" umbrella this belongs to.

PR incoming with a fix + regression tests for both triggers.

title: telemetry forget ignores RTK_DB_PATH/config.tracking.database_path, misses redirected tracking DB
author: jamesburton

title: panic: rtk gain --history crashes on non-ASCII (Cyrillic) command strings — UTF-8 char boundary
author: IlyaZaigralov
body:
## Summary

`rtk gain --history` panics with a UTF-8 char-boundary error when the recorded command string contains multi-byte characters (e.g. Cyrillic) near the history-display truncation width. The compression hook itself works fine — only the `--history` analytics view crashes.

## Panic

```
thread 'main' panicked at src/analytics/gain.rs:236:54:
byte index 22 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'с' (bytes 21..23) of `rtk proxy grep -iE успешно установлено /dev/null`
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```

## Minimal repro

```bash
# Run any rtk-proxied command whose text contains multi-byte UTF-8 (Cyrillic here)
# positioned so the ~22-byte truncation boundary lands inside a char:
rtk proxy grep -iE "успешно установлено" /dev/null

# Now view history — panics:
rtk gain --history
```

## Expected

`rtk gain --history` truncates the command string for display on a **character boundary** and prints the row (optionally with an ellipsis), regardless of the command's encoding.

## Actual

Byte-index slice at `src/analytics/gain.rs:236` cuts a multi-byte char in half → `byte index N is not a char boundary` panic → the whole `--history` command aborts. Any command with Cyrillic/emoji/CJK/accented text near the truncation width makes history permanently unviewable until that entry rotates out.

## Likely cause & fix

Line ~236 truncates with a raw byte slice like `&cmd[..N]`. Use a char-safe truncation instead, e.g.:

```rust
let shown: String = cmd.chars().take(N).collect();
// or find a safe boundary: let end = cmd.char_indices().take_while(|(i, _)| *i < N).last()...
// or the unicode-width crate for display-width-correct truncation.
```

## Environment

- rtk 0.34.3
- macOS (Darwin arm64)
- Locale with non-ASCII command output (Russian/Cyrillic)

title: Tee file generated for explicit RTK execution but not for hook-rewritten execution
author: lukasz-bienkowski
body:
## Summary

I am testing RTK tee logging with:

```toml
[tee]
enabled = true
mode = "always"
```

The most important observation is:

```text
GitHub Copilot Agent explicitly executes:

  rtk ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv

Result:
  tee file generated

GitHub Copilot Agent executes:

  ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv

Repository hook rewrites through:

  rtk hook copilot

Result:
  no tee file generated
```

The `-vvvvv` option was intentionally used to produce extensive Ansible output and rule out the explanation that tee logging was skipped because command output was too small.

This suggests a possible difference between:

```text
1. Explicit RTK command execution
2. Hook-based command rewriting through rtk hook copilot
```

The hook path is important because it is the expected transparent workflow: GitHub Copilot Agent runs normal shell commands and RTK rewrites them automatically.

I also verified that RTK is loading the configuration file correctly and that the custom settings are visible in the effective configuration.

---

## Environment

```text
RTK version: 0.43.0
Operating system: Linux
Shell: bash
Agent: GitHub Copilot Agent
Repository type: Ansible automation repository
```

Execution paths tested:

```text
1. Direct RTK command execution
2. GitHub Copilot Agent explicitly executing RTK commands
3. GitHub Copilot Agent executing normal commands rewritten by repository hook
```

---

## Configuration

RTK reports the expected configuration:

```toml
[tracking]
enabled = true
history_days = 365

[tee]
enabled = true
mode = "always"
max_files = 100
max_file_size = 52428800

[hooks]
transparent_prefixes = [
  "ansible-doc",
  "ansible-inventory",
  "ansible-galaxy"
]

[limits]
grep_max_results = 200
grep_max_per_file = 25
status_max_files = 15
status_max_untracked = 10
passthrough_max_chars = 10000
```

Additional notes:

```text
- Existing tee files were deleted during testing.
- history.db was deleted during testing.
- Deleting tee files and history.db did not restore consistent tee generation.
- max_files limit was not reached during testing.
```

---

## Configuration verification

I also verified the effective configuration using:

```bash
rtk config
```

RTK reports:

```text
Config: $HOME/.config/rtk/config.toml
```

The effective configuration shown by `rtk config` contains both:

```text
- custom configured values
- built-in default values
```

Examples of custom values visible in the effective config:

```toml
[tracking]
history_days = 365

[tee]
mode = "always"
max_files = 100
max_file_size = 52428800

[limits]
grep_max_results = 200
grep_max_per_file = 25
```

This suggests that RTK is reading `config.toml` correctly and merging it with built-in defaults.

Therefore the issue does not appear to be caused by RTK ignoring the configuration file.

Note:

```toml
directory = "/tmp/rtk"
```

was temporarily used while testing tee output location and is not part of the issue itself.

---

## Repository hook configuration

Repository-local hook:

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "command -v rtk >/dev/null 2>&1 && rtk hook copilot || true",
        "cwd": ".",
        "timeout": 5
      }
    ],
    "preToolUse": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "bash": "command -v rtk >/dev/null 2>&1 && rtk hook copilot || true",
        "powershell": "if (Get-Command rtk -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) { rtk hook copilot }",
        "cwd": ".",
        "timeoutSec": 5
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The hook appears to be active because RTK activity is visible through:

```bash
rtk gain
```

---

## Test setup

Successful playbook:

```text
test.yml
```

Failed playbook:

```text
fail.yml
```

For the key Ansible tests:

```bash
-vvvvv
```

was intentionally used to generate extensive output.

Purpose:

```text
Eliminate output volume as a possible explanation.
```

---

## Reproduction steps and results

### Test A – Small successful command

Command:

```bash
rtk bash -c 'echo hello'
```

Result:

```text
No tee file generated.
```

This may be explainable by small output size.

---

### Test B – Large successful command

Command:

```bash
rtk bash -c 'for i in {1..5000}; do echo "line $i"; done'
```

Purpose:

```text
Generate large output.
```

Result:

```text
No tee file generated.
```

This suggests output size alone does not explain the behavior.

---

### Test C – GitHub Copilot Agent explicitly invokes RTK

Command executed by GitHub Copilot Agent:

```bash
rtk ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv
```

Purpose:

```text
Run successful playbook through direct RTK invocation.
Generate large output.
```

Result:

```text
Playbook completed successfully.
Tee file generated.
```

This confirmed:

```text
- RTK is active
- tee generation can work
- successful commands can generate tee files
- large Ansible output can be logged
```

---

### Test D – GitHub Copilot Agent relies on hook rewrite

Command executed by GitHub Copilot Agent:

```bash
ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv
```

Repository hook rewrites via:

```bash
rtk hook copilot
```

Purpose:

```text
Test transparent hook-based workflow.
Use same playbook.
Use same verbosity.
```

Result:

```text
Playbook completed successfully.
Extensive output generated.
No tee file generated.
```

### Comparison

| Execution style | Command | RTK path | Output | Tee file |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Explicit RTK | `rtk ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv` | Direct wrapper | Large | YES |
| Hook rewrite | `ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv` | `rtk hook copilot` | Large | NO |

This is currently the strongest and most reproducible difference.

---

### Test E – Failed playbook

Command:

```bash
rtk ansible-playbook fail.yml
```

Result:

```text
Playbook failed.
Tee file generated.
```

Failure cases appear more likely to generate tee files.

---

## Additional observation

After tee generation worked successfully:

```text
1. Existing tee files were deleted.
2. Tests were repeated.
3. New tee files were not generated consistently.
4. history.db was deleted.
5. Behavior did not reliably recover.
```

The tee directory remained below:

```toml
max_files = 100
```

so normal rotation limits do not appear to explain the behavior.

---

## Expected behavior

With:

```toml
[tee]
enabled = true
mode = "always"
```

I expected tee logging to behave consistently.

If:

```bash
rtk ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv
```

generates a tee file,

then:

```bash
ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv
```

rewritten through:

```bash
rtk hook copilot
```

should also generate a tee file.

---

## Actual behavior

Observed:

```text
Explicit RTK execution:
  tee file generated

Hook-rewritten execution:
  no tee file generated
```

even though:

```text
- same playbook
- same verbosity (-vvvvv)
- same environment
- tee.mode = "always"
- max_files not reached
- RTK activity visible through rtk gain
- configuration loaded correctly
```

---

## Questions

1. Is tee logging expected to behave identically for:

   ```bash
   rtk ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv
   ```

   and

   ```bash
   ansible-playbook test.yml -vvvvv
   ```

   rewritten through:

   ```bash
   rtk hook copilot
   ```

2. Does `rtk hook copilot` use the same tee subsystem as direct `rtk <command>` execution?

3. Is tee logging intentionally handled differently for hook-rewritten commands?

4. If hook-based execution should generate tee files, are additional settings required?

5. Is there internal state, cache, session state, or deduplication logic that could explain:

   ```text
   tee file generated once
   subsequent executions do not generate tee files
   deleting tee files does not restore behavior
   deleting history.db does not restore behavior
   ```

6. Is there a debug or audit mode that can show:

   ```text
   - whether the hook rewrote the command
   - the final command executed
   - whether tee logging was evaluated
   - why a tee file was or was not created
   ```

---

## Why this matters

Tee files are extremely useful for GitHub Copilot Agent workflows because the agent can inspect full command output after execution without re-running commands.

Currently:

```text
Explicit RTK execution
→ tee file available

Hook-rewritten execution
→ no tee file available
```

This makes troubleshooting substantially harder because RTK may be active and `rtk gain` shows activity, but there is no tee log available for the agent to inspect afterwards.

Thank you for any clarification.

title: how install in windows???

title: Bug: rtk docker ps/logs/images reject value-taking flags (--filter, --format, --tail), fall back to raw (0% savings)
author: mahakim89
body:
## Summary
On v0.43.0, `rtk docker` rejects several standard docker flags that take values, throwing an `unexpected argument` parser error and falling back to raw output — so these high-volume commands get 0% token savings. Note the grep flag-shape fixes in v0.43.0 did not extend to the docker subcommands.

## Environment
- RTK version: rtk 0.43.0
- OS: Linux (WSL2), zsh
- Install: x86_64-unknown-linux-musl release binary

## Reproduction
Each of these prints RTK's arg-parser error and logs a `parse_failures` row (verified: counter increments by 1 per call), then falls back to raw:

```
rtk docker ps --filter name=web --format '{{.Names}}'
rtk docker logs <container> --tail 3
rtk docker logs <container> --tail=3
rtk docker images --format '{{.Repository}}'
```

Representative errors:

```
error: unexpected argument '--format' found   (Usage: rtk docker ps [OPTIONS])
error: unexpected argument '--filter' found    (Usage: rtk docker ps [OPTIONS])
error: unexpected argument '--tail' found
```

## What works (for contrast)
`rtk docker ps -a` filters correctly, so the regression is specific to value-taking flags, not docker parsing in general.

## Expected
Unrecognized/standard docker flags should be forwarded to the real docker engine (the same "run the invoked engine / never exceed raw output" fix applied to grep in v0.43.0), so filtering still applies.

## Usage data (rtk history, single machine)
- `docker ps --format ...`  : ~35 occurrences
- `docker ps --filter ...`  : ~20 occurrences
- `docker logs --tail ...`  : ~10 occurrences
- `docker compose -f ...`   : ~65 occurrences

All fell back to raw (`fallback_succeeded=1`) — no breakage, just lost savings.

## Related
- #578 (filter for `docker compose logs`) — same area, different request.

title: Hook-rewritten `git log -p | rg …` silently drops patch bodies → false-clean secret scans
author: covskycode
body:
## Summary

When the PreToolUse hook (`rtk hook claude`) rewrites a piped command like
`git log --all -p | rg '<secret-pattern>'`, the left segment becomes `rtk git log --all -p`,
whose handler replaces the patch stream with a compact summary — commit headers survive,
**patch bodies are dropped**. The downstream filter then scans a summary that no longer
contains the content being searched for, returns no matches, and exits 1.

The failure mode is the worst kind: **silent and clean-looking**. I hit it running
pre-migration secret scans over repo history — the scans reported clean; re-running with
`rtk proxy git …` surfaced a real (test-key) hit the hooked scan had missed. Anyone using
rtk with an agent that greps `git log -p` / `git diff` output (secret scans, changelog
tooling, license audits) is affected.

## Environment

- rtk 0.43.0 (Homebrew bottle), macOS 15.3 (arm64)
- Hook: Claude Code PreToolUse → `rtk hook claude`
- Also reproduces with `rtk git log --all -p | rg …` invoked directly (no hook involved)

## Minimal reproduction

```bash
mkdir /tmp/rtk-repro && cd /tmp/rtk-repro && git init -q
echo baseline > f.txt && git add f.txt && git commit -qm one
printf 'context\nSTRIPE_KEY=sk_live_FAKE1234567890\nmore\n' >> f.txt
git add f.txt && git commit -qm two

# Through rtk (what the hook rewrite produces):
rtk git log --all -p | rg -c '_KEY='     # → no output, exit 1  ❌
# Ground truth:
rtk proxy git log --all -p | rg -c '_KEY='   # → 1, exit 0      ✅
```

`rtk gain`'s history DB confirms the destruction — a hooked run over a larger repo recorded:

```
original_cmd        rtk_cmd              input_tokens  output_tokens
git log --all -p    rtk git log --all -p    1,650,152            96
```

1.65M tokens in, 96 tokens out, recorded as 99.9% "saved" — but for this use it isn't
compression, it's the data the pipeline existed to read. (Side effect: gain statistics are
inflated by runs whose output the consumer never usefully received.)

## Root cause (as of `5d32d07`)

1. **`run_log` never considers patch mode.** `src/cmds/git/git.rs` (`fn run_log`, ~L428)
   checks for format flags, limit flags, and merges — but not `-p` / `--patch` / `-u`.
   When no format flag is present it *injects* `--pretty=format:%h %s (%ar) <%an>…`,
   a default `-10` limit, and `--no-merges` ahead of the user's args, then compacts the
   captured output. Under `-p`, header lines survive that compaction but patch bodies
   don't — so the output even *looks* like git log at a glance (first lines are a real
   commit header), which makes the truncation hard to notice.
2. **The hook rewrites pipeline segments independently** (`rewrite_segment`,
   `src/discover/registry.rs`), so `git log -p | rg X` → `rtk git log -p | rg X`: the
   downstream consumer is invisible to the handler, and the handler's summarization is
   invisible to the consumer.
3. For contrast, `git stash show` *does* detect patch mode (`git.rs` ~L1748) — but still
   routes through `compact_diff(…, 100)` rather than passing through. `git diff <range>`
   piped into rg showed the same lossy behavior in my scans.

## Suggested fix

Patch-mode output is **data for a consumer**, not chrome to compress. Any of these would
resolve it (in descending order of my preference):

1. When `-p` / `--patch` / `-u` is present on `log` / `show` / `diff` / `stash show`,
   pass output through raw — at minimum when the segment is part of a pipeline (the hook
   already parses the pipeline, so it knows).
2. Never inject `--pretty` / `-10` / `--no-merges` when the user passed `-p`, `--all`,
   or explicit pathspecs — these silently change *which commits* are even inspected.
3. If summarization must stay, make dropped-content runs visible: a stderr warning
   (`rtk: summarized 1.6M → 96 tokens; use rtk proxy for raw`) would have turned a
   silent false-clean into a caught one.

## Workaround for anyone else hitting this

`~/Library/Application Support/rtk/config.toml` (macOS; `dirs::config_dir()` — note the
README's `~/.config/rtk/config.toml` path is Linux-specific):

```toml
[hooks]
exclude_commands = [
  '^git log\b.*\s-(p|u|-patch)\b',
  '^git show\b',
  '^git diff\b',
]
```

Verified: scan pipelines pass through untouched; `git status` / plain `git log`
rewrites (and their savings) are unaffected.

title: hook: leaks "no such file" stderr when probing absent dependency manifests (go.sum / package-lock.json / Cargo.lock)
author: iFredOS
body:
**Version:** 0.43.0 (Homebrew, macOS 15 / Apple Silicon)

## Summary
The Claude Code PreToolUse hook (`rtk hook claude`) probes for a dependency-manifest file based on keywords in the command being rewritten (`go` → `go.sum`, `npm` → `package-lock.json`, `cargo` → `Cargo.lock`). In a project that doesn't contain that file, the probe writes `open <path>: no such file or directory` to **stderr**, which the host (Claude Code) surfaces as "PreToolUse hook additional context" — appearing on essentially every command in projects that aren't Go/Node/Rust.

## Reproduction
```sh
cd /some/python-or-html-project   # a project with no go.sum / package-lock.json / Cargo.lock
echo '{"tool_name":"Bash","tool_input":{"command":"npm ls"},"cwd":"'"$PWD"'"}' | rtk hook claude
# → stderr: open /.../package-lock.json: no such file or directory
```
Same with a command mentioning `go` (probes `go.sum`) or `cargo` (probes `Cargo.lock`).

## Expected
A failed probe for an **optional** manifest should be silent — the file's absence is normal and expected in most projects.

## Actual
The ENOENT is written to stderr on every matching command, creating persistent noise in the host UI. Functionality is unaffected (the command rewrite still works correctly), but it's a cosmetic stderr leak that shows up on nearly every tool call in non-Go/Node/Rust projects.

## Suggested fix
Treat "manifest not found" as a non-error — swallow the ENOENT (or gate it behind a `--verbose`/debug flag) when the manifest probe is optional.

title: feat(hooks): pass side-effecting `gh pr merge` (and other mutation commands) through untouched by default
author: wjramos
body:
## Context

RTK's Claude Code PreToolUse hook (`rtk hook claude`) rewrites `gh pr merge …` into
`rtk gh pr merge …` via `hookSpecificOutput.updatedInput`. Verified on **0.42.4** (macOS, arm64):

```
$ printf '%s' '{"tool_name":"Bash","tool_input":{"command":"gh pr merge 123 --repo o/r --merge --delete-branch --match-head-commit <sha>"}}' | rtk hook claude
{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecisionReason":"RTK auto-rewrite","updatedInput":{"command":"rtk gh pr merge 123 --repo o/r --merge --delete-branch --match-head-commit <sha>"},"permissionDecision":"allow"}}
```

Up front, so this isn't mistaken for a false report: on 0.42.4 the rewrite **preserves every flag**
(`--delete-branch`, `--match-head-commit`). I ran `rtk rewrite` on the merge command 15× and all flags
survived every time. **This is a design/robustness suggestion, not a reproduced arg-stripping bug.**

## The concern

`gh pr merge` is a **side-effecting, largely irreversible** command (it merges a PR and, with
`--delete-branch`, deletes the branch). RTK offers ~no token savings here — merge output is a couple of
lines — yet it inserts itself into the execution path of a state-changing operation by rewriting the
command via `updatedInput`. Two problems that creates for downstream tooling:

1. **Validated command ≠ executed command.** A downstream PreToolUse hook (e.g. a merge gate that
   authorizes `gh pr merge` only for an independently reviewed PR@commit) validates the *original*
   command text. After RTK's `updatedInput`, the string that actually executes is `rtk gh pr merge …`.
   The gate authorized one command; another runs. The correctness of a security/merge gate shouldn't
   hinge on RTK faithfully preserving every argument of a mutation command.
2. **Fragility surface on an irreversible op.** Any change to the rewrite (or the `updatedInput`-dropping
   behavior already tracked in #1233) directly affects whether an irreversible operation runs as typed.
   For a read command a bad rewrite is a bad summary; for `gh pr merge` it's a wrong merge or an
   undeleted branch.

## Suggestion

Treat mutation / side-effecting subcommands as **pass-through by default** — no rewrite, no
`updatedInput` — for at least:

- `gh pr merge`, `gh pr close`, `gh pr create`, `gh release create` / `delete`, `gh repo delete`, …

i.e. ship a built-in equivalent of `[hooks] exclude_commands` for known side-effecting verbs, rather than
requiring every user to discover and hand-maintain `exclude_commands = ["gh pr merge"]`.

As a stopgap, `[hooks] exclude_commands = ["gh pr merge"]` (prefix match — verified on 0.42.4) suppresses
the rewrite locally.

## Related

- #2121 — built-in signal/bulk classifier for git/gh control-marker commands
- #1233 — `updatedInput` mutations silently dropped in permissive Bash modes (same mechanism, opposite failure)
- #1007 — dangerous-actions-blocker PreToolUse hook

## Environment

- rtk 0.42.4 (Homebrew, macOS arm64). Current stable 0.43.0's changelog shows no `gh pr merge` change.
- Claude Code PreToolUse hook integration (`rtk hook claude`).

title: Add an output filter for git ls-files (currently passes through unfiltered)
author: inktomi
body:
## Summary

`rtk` has no filter for `git ls-files`, so it passes through raw. `rtk discover`
flags it under "TOP UNHANDLED COMMANDS." In large repos `git ls-files`
enumerates every tracked path and can emit thousands of lines — a strong
candidate for the same token-reducing treatment rtk already applies to
`git diff` and `grep`.

## Environment

- rtk 0.43.0
- Invoked via the Claude Code `PreToolUse` hook (`rtk hook claude`)

## Why it matters

Agents routinely run `git ls-files [<pathspec>]` to enumerate tracked files
("which files under X are tracked?", "is this path tracked or ignored?"). The
output scales with repo size and is highly compressible:

- Long shared path prefixes repeat on every line.
- The caller often only needs a count, the directory structure, or a narrowed
  subset — not the full flat list.

`rtk gain` shows the biggest wins come from exactly these high-volume,
repetitive outputs (`rtk grep` is #1 by a wide margin, ~900K tokens saved).
`git ls-files` has the same shape and currently saves nothing.

## Suggested behavior (open to alternatives)

- Collapse common directory prefixes into a compact tree or per-directory
  counts, with a total tracked-file count.
- Pass through the already-tiny cases untouched — e.g. `--error-unmatch`
  single-path existence checks.
- Keep full output reachable (via a passthrough flag, or when a pathspec has
  already narrowed the result) so nothing is lost.

## Repro

```
$ rtk discover
...
TOP UNHANDLED COMMANDS -- open an issue?
Command          Count    Example
git ls-files         1    git ls-files .agents/skills/
```

Thanks — rtk's transparent hook rewriting has been great; this is the one
file-enumeration command still slipping through unfiltered.
